title: SEGMENT 4 — Anthony Atala's bladder: the first engineered organ in humans

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Anthony Atala's bladder: the first engineered organ in humans
Up until 2006, every tissue-engineered product in clinical use was a relatively flat, two-dimensional tissue — sheets of epithelium, plugs of cartilage.
They worked, but they were structurally simple.
The field had been talking, since Langer and Vacanti, about engineering whole organs, but no one had actually implanted a whole engineered organ in a human being.

That changed in April 2006, when Anthony Atala, a urologic surgeon then at Children's Hospital Boston and Harvard Medical School, published a paper in The Lancet titled "Tissue-engineered autologous bladders for patients needing cystoplasty.
" Atala and his team reported that they had built engineered bladders from the patients' own cells, implanted them, and followed the patients for years.

The work had been done on a small group of children with spina bifida, a congenital condition in which the spinal cord doesn't form properly and which often leaves children with a malfunctioning bladder.

These kids had bladders that didn't store urine properly, were causing kidney damage, and conventionally would have been treated with enterocystoplasty — surgery to enlarge the bladder using a piece of the patient's intestine.

Intestinal augmentation works, but it has problems — the intestine secretes mucus, can leak, has different mechanical properties than bladder, and is associated with metabolic and oncologic complications down the line.
Atala's approach was different.
The recipe was almost a textbook example of Langer-Vacanti.
Step one: biopsy.
Take a small piece of the patient's own bladder — too small to be functionally useful but big enough to provide a source of cells.
Step two: cell expansion.
From the biopsy, isolate two populations — urothelial cells, which line the inside of the bladder, and smooth muscle cells, which make up the bladder wall.
Expand each population separately in culture for several weeks until you have hundreds of millions of cells.
Step three: scaffold.
Make a custom-shaped biodegradable scaffold — in Atala's case, made of collagen and poly-glycolic acid — shaped to match the patient's anatomy from MRI imaging.
Step four: seeding.
Seed urothelial cells on the inside of the scaffold and smooth muscle cells on the outside.
Step five: bioreactor maturation.

Place the seeded scaffold in a bioreactor — a custom device that bathes it in nutrients and applies the right mechanical cues — for several weeks, until the cells have established a tissue-like structure on the scaffold.
Step six: implantation.
Surgically attach the engineered bladder to the patient's existing bladder remnant, replacing the dysfunctional portion.
The first patient was implanted in 1999.
The 2006 Lancet paper reported follow-up of seven patients over up to five years.
The results were encouraging.
The engineered bladders integrated with the patients' bodies.
They became vascularized — meaning blood vessels grew into them from the surrounding tissue, which is the only reason the cells survived.
They functioned, more or less, as bladders.
The patients had better bladder capacity, better compliance, better continence than they would have had without the intervention.
There were no major complications attributable to the engineered tissue.
That was the first true tissue-engineered organ implanted in humans.
Not a sheet of skin.
Not a plug of cartilage.
An organ — a three-dimensional, multi-cell-type, hollow structure that performed a specific physiological function.
The 2006 paper is a landmark in the field, and it made Atala internationally famous.
He moved to the Wake Forest Institute for Regenerative Medicine, became its director, and has been one of the most visible figures in tissue engineering ever since.
I want to flag a few things about the Atala bladder story, because in the years since 2006 the narrative has gotten complicated.
First, the patient cohort was small — seven kids — and the follow-up, while positive, has had some critics.

A few independent reviews have pointed out that the controls weren't ideal, the imaging endpoints were somewhat soft, and a subsequent attempt to commercialize the technology through a company called Tengion ran into trouble.
Tengion went bankrupt in 2014.
The bladder product did not become a routine clinical offering.

So while the 2006 paper stands as a scientific milestone, the path from "first in humans" to "approved therapy used at scale" turned out to be very difficult, for reasons that have to do as much with regulatory and commercial pressures as with the underlying biology.
Second, the bladder is, of all the major organs, arguably the easiest to engineer.
It's mostly a hollow bag with a wall of smooth muscle lined by epithelium.
It doesn't need to do complex biochemistry the way a liver does.
It doesn't need to filter the way a kidney does.
It doesn't need to beat the way a heart does.
It just needs to hold urine without leaking and let it out on cue.
The cell biology is relatively simple.
The scaffold geometry is relatively simple.
The function is relatively simple.
So the fact that Atala could make a bladder doesn't mean he, or anyone, can yet make a liver.
Third, the vascularization problem — which we'll spend a whole segment on later — was finessed in the bladder case rather than solved.
Bladders are thin-walled organs, only a few millimeters thick.
After implantation, blood vessels from the surrounding pelvic tissue could grow into the engineered bladder and supply it.
For thicker, more metabolically demanding organs, that strategy doesn't work — the cells in the middle die of hypoxia before any vessels can reach them.
Even with these caveats, the Atala bladder is a real achievement.
It demonstrated that the Langer-Vacanti recipe could scale from sheets and plugs to whole hollow organs.

It established a template — biopsy, cell expansion, scaffold, seeding, maturation, implantation — that has been applied, with variable success, to other tubular structures: urethras, blood vessels, ureters, eventually airways.
It put a face on the field.
And it remains the high-water mark of true autologous engineered-organ implantation in humans.
Nothing since has gone substantially further.

There have been bioprinted ears, bioprinted patches, lab-grown cartilage, decellularized organs reseeded with cells — but no one has implanted a tissue-engineered liver, or a tissue-engineered kidney, or a tissue-engineered heart, in a person and had it work.
The bladder is still, twenty years on, the most impressive engineered organ that has lived inside a human being.
